author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
Why Perseverance Should Be Given to One and Not Another is Inscrutable.
title: Why Perseverance Should Be Given to One and Not Another is Inscrutable.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 17 [VIII.]—Why Perseverance Should Be Given to One and Not Another is Inscrutable.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: Here, if I am asked why God should not have given them perseverance to whom He gave that love by which they might live Christianly, I answer that I do not know.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: For I do not speak arrogantly, but with acknowledgment of my small measure, when I hear the apostle saying, “O man, who art thou that repliest against God?” and, “O the depth of the riches of the wisdom and knowledge of God! how unsearchable are His judgments, and His ways untraceable!”

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: So far, therefore, as He condescends to manifest His judgments to us, let us give thanks; but so far as He thinks fit to conceal them, let us not murmur against His counsel, but believe that this also is the most wholesome for us.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: But whoever you are that are hostile to His grace, and thus ask, what do you yourself say? it is well that you do not deny yourself to be a Christian and boast of being a catholic.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: If, therefore, you confess that to persevere to the end in good is God’s gift, I think that equally with me you are ignorant why one man should receive this gift and another should not receive it; and in this case we are both unable to penetrate the unsearchable judgments of God.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: Or if you say that it pertains to man’s free will—which you defend, not in accordance with God’s grace, but in opposition to it—that any one should persevere in good, or should not persevere, and it is not by the gift of God if he persevere, but by the performance of human will, why will you strive against the words of Him who says, “I have prayed for thee, Peter, that thy faith fail not”?

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: Will you dare to say that even when Christ prayed that Peter’s faith might not fail, it would still have failed if Peter had willed it to fail; that is, if he had been unwilling that it should continue even to the end?

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: As if Peter could in any measure will otherwise than Christ had asked for him that he might will.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: For who does not know that Peter’s faith would then have perished if that will by which he was faithful should fail, and that it would have continued if that same will should abide?

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: But because “the will is prepared by the Lord,” therefore Christ’s petition on his behalf could not be a vain petition.

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: When, then, He prayed that his faith should not fail, what was it that he asked for, but that in his faith he should have a most free, strong, invincible, persevering will!

verses.book: A Treatise on Rebuke and Grace.
verses.text: Behold to what an extent the freedom of the will is defended in accordance with the grace of God, not in opposition to it; because the human will does not attain grace by freedom, but rather attains freedom by grace, and a delightful constancy, and an insuperable fortitude that it may persevere.
